channel.title: Bull & Bear With Me
channel.description: Markets, explained without the finance-bro energy. A daily 4-minute read for regular people.

item.title: Bull & Bear With Me — Apr 15, 2026
item.description:
*Banks are printing money, oil is falling, and Bitcoin just went "oh right, I do that."*
THE OPEN
Everything's up, oil's down, and for one weird Thursday in April, the market actually made sense. Enjoy it. These days don't last.
BIG THREE
- Banks had a week. JPMorgan profits up 13%. Morgan Stanley beat estimates by so much their analysts probably just lied about what the estimates were. Wells Fargo, Citi, BlackRock — all beat. When the banks are printing, the market tends to throw a party and ask questions later. That's what yesterday was.
- Oil dropped hard on Iran peace talk vibes. West Texas Intermediate — just a name for American oil, like a regional BBQ sauce — fell to around $91 a barrel after sitting above $104 earlier this month. Pakistan's army chief flew into Tehran to help broker a ceasefire extension, Trump said the war was "nearing its end," and traders immediately priced in a world where tankers can move freely again. This matters to you because gas prices and oil prices are basically the same conversation. A sustained drop here means you're not grimacing as hard at the pump by summer. Emphasis on sustained — don't go buying a truck this weekend.
- Quantum computing stocks went absolutely feral. IonQ up 21%, D-Wave up 22%, Rigetti up 13%. In one day. These are tiny companies working on computers so powerful they can theoretically solve problems regular computers can't — but they're also years away from doing anything you'd actually use. Right now they trade less like companies and more like lottery tickets. Yesterday somebody bought a lot of tickets.
ONE THING WORTH UNDERSTANDING
The Fed Chair situation is genuinely weird and worth knowing about. The Federal Reserve is the central bank — basically the entity that controls how expensive it is to borrow money in America. Your mortgage rate, your car loan, your credit card — all influenced by what those guys do. Jerome Powell has run it for years. His term ends soon. Trump nominated a guy named Kevin Warsh to replace him, and his confirmation hearing is April 21. Here's the wrinkle: one senator says he's blocking the final vote until a criminal probe into Powell gets resolved. That's not normal. Bond markets — which are basically big investors betting on future interest rates — will be watching that hearing closely. If there's uncertainty about who's in charge of the thing that controls the cost of money, markets get nervous. Fast.
KEEP AN EYE ON
- Netflix reports today. Streaming numbers, ad revenue, password-sharing crackdown update. It'll move the Nasdaq. - Fed Chair hearing on April 21. Put it in your phone. It could be genuinely weird.
THE CLOSE
S&P 500 just hit 7,000 for the first time. The Dow, somehow, was down. The Dow is 30 old companies in a trench coat pretending to be the economy. Don't @ me.
See you tomorrow.

item.title: Bull & Bear With Me — Apr 14, 2026
item.description:
*The market almost forgot there's a war on.*
THE OPEN
Green across the board, earnings coming in strong, and your 401(k) is almost back to where it was before everything went sideways. Good morning. Don't get too comfortable.
BIG THREE
- The S&P 500 is now less than one percent from its all-time high. To put that in perspective: a few weeks ago we were watching it drop like a check engine light came on. Now it's basically recovered. The Nasdaq has gone up ten days in a row — its best streak in five years. Tech carried the whole thing, which means if you've got a 401(k) sitting in any kind of growth fund, you've been quietly having a good week whether you knew it or not.
- JPMorgan crushed earnings. Goldman Sachs had their second-best quarter ever. Then Goldman's stock went DOWN. That's Wall Street doing Wall Street things — "good news already priced in" is the finance version of "yeah, but." The bank put up historic numbers and traders shrugged like a guy who just got a raise but wanted a bigger one. Exhausting people.
- CarMax dropped 15% in one day. CarMax is the big used car dealership chain, and their numbers were rough compared to last year. Worth paying attention to if you're thinking about buying or selling a car soon, because what hurts CarMax usually means the used car market is softening. Could be good news for buyers. Just noting it.
ONE THING WORTH UNDERSTANDING
Inflation came in at 3.3% last month. Here's what that means in plain English: a thing that cost $100 a year ago now costs $103.30. The main culprit is gas prices — energy swings around a lot, which is why economists also look at "core inflation," which just strips out food and gas to get a cleaner read. Core came in at 2.6%. The Fed — the guys who set interest rates for the whole country — want inflation closer to 2% before they'll cut rates. Cutting rates is basically the Fed making it cheaper to borrow money, which tends to get the economy moving. At 3.3%, they're not doing that yet. There's a 97% chance they hold steady at their meeting on April 28th. So don't hold your breath for cheaper mortgages or car loans anytime soon.
KEEP AN EYE ON
- The Strait of Hormuz situation. That's the narrow waterway where a huge chunk of the world's oil moves through. The U.S. blockaded Iranian oil shipments there. One Wall Street CEO said if it stays shut for six to twelve months, the whole world goes into a recession. Iran apparently called Washington. Something's happening. We just don't know what yet.
- Jerome Powell is out as Fed chair on May 15th. His replacement has crypto in his personal portfolio. Make of that what you will.
THE CLOSE
Your retirement account recovered. Inflation is still annoying. There's a naval blockade happening. It's a lot for a Tuesday. Go touch grass.

item.title: Bull & Bear With Me — Apr 13, 2026
item.description:
**📈 The Open**
The U.S. just blockaded the Strait of Hormuz, oil hit $104 a barrel, inflation jumped to 3.3%, Goldman missed on bonds, and somehow the S&P is basically flat. Markets are fine. Everything is fine.
---
**🎯 Big Three**
- **The Strait of Hormuz is blockaded.** About 20% of the world's oil flows through that narrow waterway, and we just put a Navy cork in it. Gas was already $4.12 a gallon before this. Analysts are calling it the worst energy disruption since the 1970s, which is a hell of a thing to read before your Monday morning coffee.
- **Inflation jumped from 2.4% to 3.3% in one month.** That's not a drift. That's a lurch. The Fed has two jobs — keep inflation down and keep employment up — and right now both are quietly going sideways. Their response, per Vice Chair Jefferson: our policy is "well-positioned." Comforting stuff, truly.
- **Goldman Sachs had a near-record quarter and still dropped 4%.** Revenues up 14%. EPS beat. But the bond desk missed by $850 million and Wall Street punished them like they showed up to the company picnic without potato salad. The rest of the big banks — JPMorgan, Wells Fargo, Citi, BofA — report this week. Could be the most expensive week of earnings season to actually pay attention to.
---
**🧠 One Thing Worth Understanding**
**The Strait of Hormuz, and why it matters to your drive to work.**
The Strait of Hormuz is a narrow strip of water between Iran and Oman — about 21 miles wide at its tightest point. Something like 20-21% of all the world's oil passes through it every single day, tankers carrying crude from Saudi Arabia, Iraq, Kuwait, and the UAE to the rest of the planet. When that flow gets disrupted — whether by conflict, blockade, or threats — oil prices spike globally because traders don't wait around to see how it plays out. They panic-buy futures and the price jumps immediately, which is why WTI crude went up 8% in one morning. That feeds into gas prices within days, then into shipping costs, then into basically everything that gets transported or made with energy, which is everything. The 1970s oil crisis caused recessions across the Western world. We're not there yet. But the direction of travel is not great, Bob.
---
**👀 Keep An Eye On**
- **Bank earnings all week.** JPMorgan reports Tuesday and tends to set the tone. If they miss, expect the "soft landing" crowd to go quiet for a few days. - **Oil prices Monday night and Tuesday.** Any further escalation around the blockade could push WTI toward $110. That's the number that really starts showing up in everything.
---
**☕ The Close**
Markets are "pricing in the geopolitical risk," which is analyst-speak for "we have no idea what happens next, but we've agreed to be calm about it." Respect the process.
